text: (from 1628–1649), Descartes’ ideas inevitably provoked those with Orthodox Calvinist ideas, and Descartes was not wise enough to stay out of trouble in his host country, where he eventually became looked upon with suspicion as well (Watson 2007: 221–43). However, beyond the substantial debates that brought Descartes into conflict with official doctrines (Catholic and Calvinist alike) one can detect an underlying religious inspiration to his problématique: Descartes’ problem was how to make statements about the existence of the world that can be taken as absolute truth. ‘Error’ to Descartes was a kind of evil, and Gellner (1992) is surely right in indicating how Descartes’ understanding of the problem was deeply religious in nature: what tormented Descartes was not that he might be damned, but that he might be mistaken (Gellner 1992: 13). Descartes’ language is on this point most direct in Meditations on First Philosophy, and especially in the Fourth Meditation, Of the true and the false, where he tackles the problem of ‘error’. Descartes talks about the will that ‘easily falls into error and sin, and chooses the evil for the good, or the false for the true’ (1997: 166), putting thus truth and good, and error and evil on the same plane. True enough, in his own introduction to his Meditations (ibid.: 123–33), Descartes is keen to stress (before his Catholic audience) that the ‘sin’ he will talk about relates only to errors in deciding between the true and false, and not to questions of faith (ibid.: 132); yet his drawing on the same vocabulary is at the same stroke further underlined; and Descartes, as we know, will not refrain from proving the existence of God. This drove Descartes to the question: why did God put error into the world? The question is identical to another question, which God’s children normally address from an early age: why did God put evil into the world? If God decides everything, why is there evil? The believer is puzzled and arrives at the conclusion that either evil was some kind of secret force that slipped out of God’s control (Satan as the jealous angel), or that God (since He is, after all, almighty) let evil loose to punish mankind. Descartes found no satisfaction in either answer. Indeed, he found such answers part of the error he wanted to undo. He proved that error had not been God’s intention at all. Error had come about by an erroneous path taken towards cognitive truth, and in his Fourth Meditation Descartes claims to have set us back on the right track of true reason (ibid.: 166–9). Having singled out error as the demon, Descartes wanted to establish some ground on which error was defeated by truth. That ground was his self. From there, Descartes went on to redesign himself out of a solitary vision. His road to liberation went through a refuge into self, and from this refuge he could reach salvation from error. Knowledge saves. Although one should not take such statements at face value, Descartes himself claimed that from an early age he received signs concerning some kind of revelation of truth, that he would dedicate his life to the pursuit of truth, and that his possible success in this self-imposed task would serve as a new basis of universal order and knowledge (see Rodis-Lewis 1992). Liminality in the Transition to Modernity 123 Descartes and the New Ground of Order A few further points about Descartes and his new philosophy must be made explicit at this point. Descartes can surely be considered a Weberian ‘switchman’: he is one of those thinkers who managed to change the shape of the subject before him. Weber invoked the metaphor of switchmen in the context of his comparative study of the world religions (1948), invoking the prophet as ideal type. Weber never worked out a full-blown typology for intellectual figures, but in so many ways a thinker like Descartes performs as a prophet: he diagnoses the illnesses of his age, establishes a new truth through inward reflexivity, and then offers the truth to the world as a revelatory key to a new epoch. He posits himself as the medium through which the entire world can bring about a change. The radical nature of Descartes’ philosophy is visible by the way in which he ‘clears the ground’ by totally erasing the relevance of pre-existing knowledge and experience. In The Passions of the Soul Descartes characteristically declared that he feels obliged to write ‘as though I were treating of a matter which no one had ever touched on before me’ (1997: 358). Descartes’ attack on almost all existing knowledge is repeated elsewhere. In The Principles of Philosophy, first published in 1644, Descartes dismantled what he here called the ‘prejudices of childhood’ (1997: 305), the source of all errors: our reliance on sense-impressions. And also here Descartes proceeded to replace the world of errors with a new-found certainty, the cogito. The gnostic thinker creates a void so that he can erect a new edifice from scratch and bestow order on the world – the whole world. How can one understand Descartes’ ‘ordering experience’? The exact road taken by Descartes towards pure reason is not our issue here. It went through doubt, and it went through absolute divisions of body and mind, faith and reason. In Discourse on Method, Descartes had argued that the self was ‘completely distinct from the body’ and could exist without it (Descartes 1999: 25). Paving the way for the cogito, this was the much known and criticized mind-body dualism that Descartes installed. Indeed, Descartes was openly taking departure from the Aristotelian account of soul which denied the mental any reification as separate substance. In this sense, Descartes returned to Plato’s notion of the soul as separate from the body, while at the same time severely delimiting what ‘soul’ means. 4 To Plato, the soul was composed of logos, thymos and eros, and humans should strive towards reaching a balance between these dimensions of reason, emotion
